evidence_quotes.quote: destroy any copy of the games they shut down.

paragraphs.text: The games industry has been working in grey zones for far too long. A lot of what they are doing is probably already illegal, but no singular consumer has the time or money to fight them on this. Publishers and game studios should not be allowed to dictate how long we can play a game for. Sure online servers cost money, and therefore we can not expect them to run them forever. But its not that hard to allow the community to take over afterwards. server software, or even just a damn description of what the server expect on a high level. And a gurante that the publisher/company cant come after you for writing your own server code. Would go a long way for preserving games. The industry also keeps making offline games, online only. even tho they dont need to be. the best example is the Crew, which was recently killed. There is absolutely no reason why it needed to be tethered to online only in the first place. sure the multiplayer part does need the servers. But the rest of the game wouldve run perfectly offline too. Now Ubisoft even wants to go further, writing in theyr Terms of service, that we are to destroy any copy of the games they shut down. Imagine if A car manufacturer tells you to go ahead and destroy your car, because they no longer make it. That is pure madness. Games have been working fine, and the greed of publishers/ the games industry as a whole keeps pushing them to make these ridicules anti consumer moves. We get sold licenses instead of actual copies. And until recently this was barely even visible. Completely misleading consumers. The consumers hate this. The Stop killing games, citizens initiative rightfully has over 1 millions signups. And even the publishers have begun to muddy the waters, by trying to discredit it. They are scared, and they should be. The times of them doing whatever they please need to stop. Theyr practices need to be checked and reigned in. Sincerly, a worried eu citizen.
